coredata.dc:title: Positioning of functional group for tailoring absorption spectrum of carbon dots: Insights from density functional theory
coredata.dc:creator: Umami, Reza
Permatasari, Fitri Aulia
Sundari, Citra Deliana Dewi
Santika, Arum Sinda
Iskandar, Ferry
coredata.dc:description: This research provided a comprehensive theoretical investigation into the tailoring of absorption in carbon dots (CDs) through the positioning of functional groups (amino and carbonyl) on their surfaces, employing Density Functional Theory (DFT). This study not only unveils the underlying physical mechanisms governing the absorption properties of CDs but also provides valuable insights into the optimal positioning of functional groups to achieve redshift absorption. A detailed analysis of the electronic structure provided a profound understanding of the intricate relationship between the optical behaviors exhibited by the system's CDs. By strategically arranging these functional groups, the CDs' electric circular dichroism (ECD) spectra were unique, which might coherently indicate the molecular chirality behavior in the CDs system. This paves the way for exciting possibilities in their potential biomedical and optoelectronic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
DFT
Chirality
NIR absorption
Position

R.U, A.A.S. and C.D.D.S. would like to thank the Ministry of Fiscal Indonesia Endowment Fund for Education (LPDP) for their doctoral scholarship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The optimized geometrical structure of CDs. (a) Amino functionalized CDs in various amino positions (1,3 amino CDs, 1,4 amino CDs, 1,6 amino CDs, 1,11 amino CDs and 1,13 amino CDs). (b) Amino carbonyl functionalized CDs in various positions (1 amino CDs CO, 9 amino CDs CO, 11 amino CDs CO, 12 amino CDs CO and 16 amino CDs CO).

The calculated absorbance spectra of amino and carbonyl functionalized CDs. (a) amino functionalized CDs in varying amino positions. (b) Amino-carbonyl functionalized CDs in varying amino to carbonyl positions. The Gaussian broadening of peaks is σ = 45 nm. The inset picture is the magnified spectra in the 400–700 nm range.

The electronic circular dichroism (ECD) spectra of amino and carbonyl functionalized CDs system. (a) Amino functionalized CDs in varying positions. (b) Amino-carbonyl functionalized CDs in varying positions.

The calculated HOMO and LUMO energy levels of amino and carbonyl functionalized CDs. (a) Amino-functionalized CDs in varying amino positions. (b) Amino carbonyl functionalized CDs in varying amino to carbonyl positions. Yellow and dark blue lines indicate the HOMO and LUMO levels, respectively. Inset the hole-electron distribution upon excitation of CDs system in HOMO and LUMO levels (the iso-value is 0.03, and the red and purple represent hole and electron, respectively). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The heat map of the transition density matrix of CDs. (a) Amino-functionalized CDs in various amino positions. (b) Amino carbonyl functionalized CDs in various amino carbonyl positions.

The charge difference density (CDD) in excitation state S1. The yellow and green represent holes and electrons, respectively. The contour value is
3 x 10
− 4
. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The stereochemical configuration and their first ECD signal speak of the studied CDs system.

The parameter of chemical descriptor values energy gap (Eg, eV), hardness (
η , eV
)
, chemical potential (
μ , eV
), and electrophilicity index
( ω )
.

head.ce:title: Positioning of functional group for tailoring absorption spectrum of carbon dots: Insights from density functional theory

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research provided a comprehensive theoretical investigation into the tailoring of absorption in carbon dots (CDs) through the positioning of functional groups (amino and carbonyl) on their surfaces, employing Density Functional Theory (DFT). This study not only unveils the underlying physical mechanisms governing the absorption properties of CDs but also provides valuable insights into the optimal positioning of functional groups to achieve redshift absorption. A detailed analysis of the electronic structure provided a profound understanding of the intricate relationship between the optical behaviors exhibited by the system's CDs. By strategically arranging these functional groups, the CDs' electric circular dichroism (ECD) spectra were unique, which might coherently indicate the molecular chirality behavior in the CDs system. This paves the way for exciting possibilities in their potential biomedical and optoelectronic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are an emergent luminescent carbon-based nanomaterials with size below 10 nm [1,2]. The CDs are highly promising because of their facile synthesis, multicolor emission, excellent biocompatibility and easy functionalization [3–5]. These CDs’ superior properties open broad potential applications, including biomedicine, light harvesting, and catalysts. Various attempts have been dedicated in computational and experimental fields to engineering the CDs structures for enhanced properties and exploring new properties, including heteroatom doping [6–10], Nitrogen configuration [11–14], increasing the sp2 domain size [15–17], and surface functionalization [18–20], and chirality utilization of CDs [21,22].

In recent studies, the tailoring of optical properties' CDs to achieve redshift absorption is fully exploited theoretically and experimentally. Theoretical investigations play a pivotal role in unraveling the underlying physical mechanisms governing the absorption properties of CDs and providing valuable insights for their design and optimization with a low cost and short time. Density Functional Theory (DFT) has proven to be a powerful tool for studying nanomaterials' electronic structure and optical properties, offering a detailed understanding of the interactions between molecules and light. The investigations utilizing time dependent-density functional theory (TD-DFT) calculation to modulate absorption redshift through laying down the functional group in polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAHs) molecule have been reported, but there are no regularities positioning and did not regard the interaction between functional group [18,23–25]. The interaction of homogenous (amino-amino) and heterogeneous (amino-carbonyl) plays a crucial role in the electron-hole pathway in the core system of CDs.

Our groups have revealed that surface functionalization is one of the effective ways to modify the electronical and optical properties of CDs through various surface functional groups and their combinations, concentration, and position [19,26–28]. Therefore, it is highly intriguing to elucidate the surface functional groups position and their influence on the absorption shifting of CDs. Our primary objectives are two-fold. Firstly, we seek to unveil the fundamental principles that govern the absorption properties of CDs, providing a profound understanding of the intricate relationship between their electronic structure and optical behaviors. Secondly, we aim to provide valuable insights into achieving redshift absorption, which is crucial for expanding the spectral range of CDs' optical response, by strategically positioning functional groups on their surfaces. These studies could open a new avenue to innovatively design CDs with controllable absorption spectrum for any application, especially biomedical field. Furthermore, due to their high biocompatibility, the amino and carbonyl groups that could be utilized as chemical tools are preferable in biomedical applications.

In this work, the CDs model compound was designed based on polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) decorated with amino (-NH2) and carbonyl (CO) functional groups in the surface CDs with a variety of configurations. All calculations were performed using density functional theory (DFT) in the Gaussian 09 software package. The ground-state of geometries was optimized in a water environment using a polarizable continuum model (PCM) with Becke three-parameter Lee−Yang−Parr (B3LYP) hybrid function and 6-311G** basis set. The energies, oscillator strength and absorption spectra were calculated within the time-dependent (TD-DFT/B3LYP(6-311G**) approach. The non-appearance of negative frequencies confirmed the stability of the molecular structures. All optical and electronic properties were rendered by Multiwfn [29] and Chemcraft 1.8. [30], while the ECD spectra were visualized using GaussView [31].

Surface functionalization is an effective approach to tailoring the optic and electronic properties of CDs [25,32–34]. The pristine CDs were modeled as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), and the surface structure of CDs was expected as well-designed by the presence of amino (-NH3) and carbonyl (CO) functional groups. Experimentally, the presence of these functional groups has been simultaneously manifested on the CDs’ surface, derived from their precursor (e.g., citric acid and urea) [14,35–37]. As observed in our previous research, the presence of oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on CDs can be achieved via a bottom-up approach using precursors, citric acid and urea [1,19,26–28,35,38]. The formation of these functional groups has been elucidated based on a comprehensive analysis of FTIR and XPS spectra [2,38]. In brief, citric acid amide was formed through the reaction of citric acid and urea. Upon subsequent heating, citric acid amide self-assembles into nanosheet structures through dehydration and deammoniation involving carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups under hydrothermal conditions [38]. Then, the surface CDs that conducted amino-amino and amino-carbonyl groups in various positions were evaluated, as shown in Fig. 1 . The interaction amino-amino has been led in amino-to-amino group distance in the range of 0.27 nm–1.43 nm (Fig. 1a). Furthermore, the interplay of subunit amino-carbonyl was evaluated in varying amino-to-carbonyl distance in a range of 0.26 nm–1.35 nm (Fig. 1b).

The presence of functional group amino and carbonyl in surface CDs is adequate to alter the optical properties, especially absorption. It has been clearly shown that the amino and carbonyl position could also tailor their absorption spectra, as shown in Fig. 2 . In general, the spectra absorption of pristine CDs generates two typical absorption peaks, around 250 nm and 440 nm (Fig. S1), which closely resemble the experimental data (Fig. S2). The main peak at ∼250 nm and ∼440 nm indicated the transition π-π*, which corresponded to the CC bond, and the n-π* transition that attributed to the surface functional C–N/CO, respectively [35,39,40]. As amino-to-amino distance increased, the peak of absorbance low-energy regions shifted blue. Consequently, the absorption peak of the amino-to-amino distance from 0.27 nm to 1.43 nm was also shifted from 603 nm to 592 nm (Fig. 2a). A similar circumstance was also reported for model pyrene and anthracene-based CDs doping amino [25]. The Nitrogen functional group, such as pyrrolic, pyridinic, graphitic and amino, has been commonly used as surface functional to obtain the redshift absorption of CDs [19,23,41–43]. Intriguingly, a shifted red in varying amino-to-carbonyl distance was generated due to the interaction of amino carbonyl (Fig. 2b). The absorption peak was redshift from 661 to 761 nm by the increasing of the amino to carbonyl distance from 0.26 to 1.35 nm. Accordingly, it is in line with other works that the position functional group in CDs system (pyrene and anthracene) is essential for gaining long-wavelength red emission CDs. The correlation is universal across various systems comprising polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons compound and electron-donating groups [25].

The functional groups' positioning could also induce a distinguish the Electric Circular Dichroism (ECD) spectra, as shown in Fig. 3 . It might be coherently indicated that the functional groups' positioning induces the CD system's chirality behavior, as reported elsewhere [44]. It is noteworthy that pristine and other functional groups did not exhibit the ECD spectra (Fig. S3 and Fig. S4). It indicated that the amino and carbonyl groups could be utilized as chemical tools to induce the chirality behavior of CDs. Based on the functionalized CDs' ECD spectra, the exhibited ECD spectra could be categorized into the chiroptical activity of the L- and D-chiral systems, reflecting their tendency towards a specific stereoisomer. The main ECD signal peak for the amino-amino and amino-to-carbonyl functional groups are 220–240 nm and 260–270 nm, respectively. The positive and negative signals of these ECD spectra in the CD system indicate that the L and D are based on stereochemical configuration. The first negative signal peak of the D-CDs is consistently in the longer wavelength than the first positive signal peak of the L-CDs (Fig. 3a). In addition, the amino-carbonyl CDs system also showed lower intensity in longer wavelength than the amino CDs system. The first signal peak of the ECD spectra of amino-carbonyl CDs is redshift (∼40 nm) as increasing their amino-to-carbonyl distance (Fig. 3b). The stereochemical configuration of the amino carbonyl functionalized CDs system is summarized in Table 1 . It indicates that the positioning of the amino carbonyl functional group might be coherent to induce and dictate the stereochemical configuration of a chiral molecule. Indeed, the controllable stereochemical configuration of chiral could be beneficial for biomedical applications, including imaging and drug delivery [21,45]. Moreover, the stereochemical configuration might be plausible to alter the circularly polarized luminescent (CPL) properties, as observed in other organic materials [46–48]. These CPL properties could broaden potential applications in optoelectronic devices.

To provide a more comprehensive understanding of the correlation between optical and electronic properties influenced by amino-amino and amino-carbonyl interaction in a CDs system, the energy levels of the studied CDs were calculated using DFT calculation. The energy gap was defined as the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy. Fig. 4 shows the energy level diagram of the CDs system in the Fermi region and their respective hole and electron distribution. The bandgap energy gradually increases from 2.43 to 2.52 eV, as increasing the amino-to-amino distance from 1,3 amino CDs to 1,13 amino CDs (Fig. 4a), which could also induce a blueshift absorption peak in the lower energy (Fig. 2a). Significant shifting was observed in the presence of carbonyl (CO) in the CDs system (Fig. 4b), induced by lower HOMO levels that might be plausibly attributed to the highly polarized π bond [49]. The carbonyl functional group comprises an oxygen atom bonded to a carbon atom through a double bond, which forms a π bond. This bond is highly polarized, wherein the oxygen atom carries a negative charge while the carbon atom carries a positive charge. Consequently, the electrons in the π bond are localized mainly on the oxygen atom, lowering the LUMO energy level.

The underlying mechanism behind CDs' tailoring shift absorption and gap energy was conducted through an atomic orbital contribution analysis (Table S1). The molecular orbital component analysis showed that amino-amino position interaction contributes significantly at H → L with a percentage of more than 70%. The role of the Nitrogen atom in the amino functional group is as a donating electron in occupied levels. As the amino-to-amino distance increases, the atomic contribution of Nitrogen atoms in occupied levels also increases. On the other hand, the amino-carbonyl significantly contributed at H-1 → L with a percentage of more than 90%. The role of the Oxygen atom in the carbonyl functional group is to accept electrons in unoccupied levels. This atomic contribution also increases with the increase of amino to carbonyl distances.

The contribution value of charge transfer between atoms and fragments in excited states can be thoroughly examined using the heatmap transition density matrix, as depicted in Fig. 5 . The transition density matrix (TDM) is an effective tool for understanding electronic excitation processes in molecular systems. In the case of the amino-functionalized CDs system, the holes were highly localized in the amino fragment, indicating the electron donor behavior of amino groups to the core system (Fig. 5a). These donating electron behaviors of amino groups were also clearly observed in amino carbonyl functionalized (Fig. 5b). However, in the presence of the carbonyl groups in system's CDs, the amino groups were donating their electron to the specific carbonyl fragment. This observation aligns with our previous research that the amino and carbonyl groups tend to possess donating and withdrawing electron behaviors, respectively [26,49].

The charge distribution and electron density differences between the ground and excited states can be analyzed using charge difference density (CDD), as shown in Fig. 6 . The investigation of CDD reveals that the amino functional group tends to donate electrons, whereas the carbonyl functional group demonstrates an inclination to accept electrons. In the case of an amino-functionalized CDs system, electronic charge distribution becomes increasingly delocalized as the distance increases. This observation is supported by the smallest charge transfer length (CTL) value, as indicated in Table 1. Generally, the delocalized charge in the CDD map corresponds to a reduced charge transfer length (CTL), facilitating more efficient charge transfer processes and resulting in transitions occurring at higher energy levels [25,50]. This phenomenon enables the occurrence of a blue shift in the absorbance spectrum.

In contrast, in carbonyl groups, specifically oxygen bonds (=O), the CTL value is longer than amino CDs (exceeds 2 Bohr), indicating a localized charge distribution within the CDs system. It was attributed to the donor behavior of amino and the accepting behavior of carbonyl. The increasing distance between the amino and carbonyl functional groups results in a greater CTL distance, leading to transitions occurring at lower energy levels, manifested in redshift absorption.

In any biomedical application, chemical reactivity and sensitivity are pivotal. Subsequently, an assessment was conducted on the overall descriptive parameters of a molecule to gain insights into the connection between chemical reactivity, stability, and reactions to variations in external circumstances, as tabulated in Table 2 . The global descriptive parameters comprise the hardness, chemical potential and electrophilicity index. The stability of structure CDs was determined through its electrophilicity index (ω). The electrophilicity index is a metric for deciding stability [41,51].
(1)
Electrophilicity
index
( ω )
=
μ 2
2 η
where η and μ represent the hardness and chemical potential, respectively, these values depend on the energies of the LUMO and HOMO.
(2)
Hardness
( η )
=
E
L U M O
−
E
H O M O
2
and
(3)
Chemical
potential
( μ )
= −
E
H O M O
−
E
L U M O
2

The electrophilicity index indicates the chemical stability of the molecule when it receives an extra electrical charge. If the chemical hardness is low, the electrophilicity (ω) increases. The electrophilicity index of amino-functionalized CDs is lower than that of amino-carbonyl-functionalized CDs (Table 2). It indicates that the amino-functionalized CDs are more chemically stable than the amino-carbonyl-functionalized CDs. However, a high electrophilicity index of the amino-carbonyl-functionalized CDs could be inferred their beneficial for the feasible electronic transport and conductivity [51]. Beyond that, studies imply that the functional group position of CDs could tailor their electronic system, stability, solubility, and molecular packing, which provides a significant degree of freedom in designing various applications, such as biosensing, drug delivery agents, or high-performance organic semiconductors [52–57].

In summary, this study focused on well designing of carbon dots (CDs) facilitated by the functional groups positioning such as amino and carbonyl. We systematically evaluated the positioning of the amino-amino and amino-carbonyl interactions within CDs by employing TD-DFT calculations. The donor-acceptor behavior of the amino and carbonyl dictates the CD system's charge transfer. The amino to carbonyl distance could manipulate the CD system's charge transfer and alter its energy level. Consequently, the absorption spectra of CDs could be tailored, both redshift and blue shift. Notably, the strategic positioning distance of the amino-carbonyl functional group (0.26 nm–1.35 nm) enabled the tuning of redshift absorption spectra worth (∼100 nm).

Conversely, short distances (0.27 nm–1.43 nm) between amino-amino functional groups caused a blueshift effect, shifting the absorption peak from 603 nm to 592 nm in correlation with variations in the HOMO and LUMO energy levels. These findings also might induce the chirality behavior through the Electric Circular Dichroism (ECD) spectra. Precisely adjusting the position of functional groups (amino and carbonyl) in surfaces CDs system can alter their stereochemical configuration.

Reza Umami: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Fitri Aulia Permatasari: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Conceptualization. Citra Deliana Dewi Sundari: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Arum Sinda Santika: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Ferry Iskandar: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is fully supported by Riset Dosen Tidak Tetap Peneliti (DTTP), Institute for Science and Technology Development Institut Teknologi Bandung, Equity Program 2024, Grant Number: 299/IT1.B07.5/TA/2024.. R.U, A.A.S. and C.D.D.S. would like to thank the Ministry of Fiscal Indonesia Endowment Fund for Education (LPDP) for their doctoral scholarship.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design of pyrrolic-N-rich carbon dots with absorption in the first near-infrared window for photothermal therapy
